title: Pokemon Dashboard
text:
This dashboard is not original as it has been edited with AI
A friend came over in May 2026 and mentioned that their kid liked Pokemon and was playing the new competitive game. As someone who played competitive VGC (competitive format) back in the early 2010’s, I was excited and thought it would be fun to make a dashboard and dive into some pokemon data. Unfortunately, playing the game wasn’t quite as fun as when I was a teenager, but it did make for a fun bonding moment.
This interactive dashboard runs entirely in your browser using Shinylive, meaning there is no server side to do the computations. Due to this, the first load may take a moment.
Open the dashboard in a new tab

title: Metagenomic Analysis of Fecal Samples as a Differential Diagnostic Tool for Colorectal Cancer
Metagenomic Analysis of Fecal Samples as a Differential Diagnostic Tool for Colorectal Cancer
Predicting League of Legends Matches
text:
Incomplete, please go here to see more of this project.

Introduction
League of Legends is a MOBA (Multiplayer Online Battle Arena) where two teams (Blue and Red) of five players compete over each other’s resources and neutral objectives to destroy the enemy base (Nexus). There are three lanes; Top, Middle (Mid), and Bottom (Bot), each with defensive structures (Towers or Turrets); a jungle, filled with neutral monsters that give gold, experience and some give buffs (power-ups); a River with epic neutral objectives that provide permanent buffs (Void Grubs and Dragons) and some that provide temporary boosts to your team (Elder Dragon, Baron and Rift Herald). After the 15 minute mark, teams have the option to surrender (colloquially known as “ff” for “forfeit”). Often players who are underperforming will want to surrender to save time. Can we predict which team will win so into the game?

Data Collection
This data was collected using the Riot Games API using two different calls. First I needed to collect a list of diamond players, which was done using the /lol/league/v4/entries/{queue}/{tier}/{division} endpoint. From the players collected I used their puuid’s to ping /lol/match/v5/matches/{matchId}/timeline. This returns a minute by minute recap of the game in JSON format. I then cleaned the JSONs and then saved the data of the first 15 minutes of each game in a PostgreSQL table.


Content
This dataset contains game metrics of the first 15 minutes of a match–games usually last between 25-35 minutes, but can surpass an hour. The data collected represents approximately 20,000 games of ranked solo queue (players can only queue up for a game with maximum one other person) from 2024 Diamond Players (n.b. Patch 14.6, all games are from between March 20th and March 31st, 2024). The target value is blue_Wins (1 represents a Blue Team Victory, 0 represents a Red Team Victory)
Each game is unique and there should be no missing values, match_id can be used to GET more data from the Riot Games API.


Glossary
Wards: The in game map includes Fog of War, which means players can only see what is in their immediate vicinity. Wards are totems that players can drop to temporarily or permanently gain vision in areas.

Yellow Wards: Usable after level 1, a player may have up to 3 activate at any time, can be placed up to 600 units away, last 90-120 seconds (based on average champion level) or until they are destroyed (3 HP).
Sight Wards: Functionally the same as a Yellow Ward, usually placed by Supports (Please correct if wrong)
Blue Wards: Only usable after level 9, a player may only have 1 active at any time, can be placed up to 4000 units away, last until they are destroyed (1 HP).
Control Wards: Purchasable single-use wards available from level 1, a player may only have 1 activate at any time. Reveals invisible units, last until they are destroyed (4 HP)

Ace: When a team kills all members of the enemy team such that all enemy players are dead at a given moment.
Assist: When a player helps secure a kill (but did not give the killing blow themself), either by damaging, using crowd control (slowing, stunning, etc), or buffing (healing, powering up) an ally that also participated in the kill.
Void Grub: A Neutral Epic Monster that gives players an increase in damage to enemy structures (Towers/Turrets, Inhibitors) increasing for every extra Void Grub defeated. Only 3 or 6 will spawn in a game, but not all need to be or will be killed. n.b. if a team kills 5 or 6 Void Grubs, they will periodically summon 1 or 2 Voidmites (little alien things) that assist in destroying objects).
Dragons: Neutral Epic Monster that grant permanent bonuses to a team. Only 3 types of non-Elder Dragons (Not included in this dataset as Elder cannot appear prior to 15 minutes) will spawn in a given game. 1. Cloud Drake: makes players faster 2. Infernal Drake: increases player attack damage and ability power 3. Mountain Drake: increases player armor and magic resistance 4. Ocean Drake: gives players extra health regeneration 5. Chemtech Drake: grants players resistance to crowd control and extra healing and shielding 6. Hextech Drake: increases the rate at which players can attack and use spells
Herald: A Neutral Objective that assists players in destroying enemy structures
Towers/Turrets: “Tower” and “Turret” are used interchangeably, these are defensive structures that must be destroyed to reach the enemy Nexus. There are 3 in each lane, and 2 at the enemy Nexus. They give gold and have plates
Turret Plates: The first/outer turrets in each lane have 5 plates that grant extra resistances to the tower for the first 14 minutes. Each plate destroyed prior to 14 minutes grants additional gold.
Inhibitor: A structure at the enemy base, destroying this gives your minions additional power and at least one must be destroyed to destroy the enemy Nexus. They regenerate after a few minutes.
Gold: A resource earned by killing minions, jungle monsters, enemy players and destroying neutral objectives, enemy structures, wards. Gold is used to buy items to increase player strength.
XP: XP and Experience used interchangeably–A resource earned by killing minions, jungle monsters, enemy players and destroying neutral objectives, enemy structures. XP allows a player to level-up, granting additonal strength and allowing players to learn new and upgrade abilities.
Level: Champion Level, starts at 1, maximum of 18.
CS: Creep Score / Minions. Each team spawns minions in their lanes. Creep score is a count of how many minions a team has killed, (they must land the final blow on a creep to gain gold, but can gain XP by just being in the vicinity when one dies).
Jungle Monsters: Outside of the Lanes is the Jungle, filled with neutral monsters that grant gold, XP and some grant buffs



Can you Predict the Winning Team at 15 Minutes?


Show Code
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import os
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, GridSearchCV
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report, confusion_matrix, roc_curve, roc_auc_score
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
import xgboost as xgb
df = pd.read_csv("LoL_15_Diamond.csv")

5 rows × 89 columns




Simplifying the Dataset
Rather than train a model on win/loss with blue vs red features, we are calculating differences between blues score and red score for each feature so that the model will learn from relative advantages rather than individual teams’ values. Positive values will indicate that the blue team has a higher score for that metric, and negative will indicate that the blue team has a lower score.


Show Code
columns_to_keep = ['blue_Win', 'blue_Wards', 'blue_Wards_Destroyed', 'blue_First_Blood',
                   'blue_Kills', 'blue_Aces', 'blue_Assists', 'blue_Void_Grubs', 'blue_Dragons',
                   'blue_Heralds', 'blue_Towers_Destroyed', 'blue_Turret_Plates', 'blue_Inhibitors',
                   'blue_Total_CS', 'blue_Total_Jungle_Monsters', 'blue_Gold_Diff', 'blue_XP_Diff',
                   'blue_Total_Dmg', 'red_Wards', 'red_Wards_Destroyed', 'red_Kills', 'red_Aces',
                   'red_Assists', 'red_Void_Grubs', 'red_Dragons', 'red_Heralds', 'red_Towers_Destroyed',
                   'red_Turret_Plates', 'red_Inhibitors', 'red_Total_CS','red_Total_Jungle_Monsters', 'red_Total_Dmg']

df = df[columns_to_keep]

df['Ward_Diff'] = df['blue_Wards'] - df['red_Wards']
df['Wards_Destroyed_Diff'] = df['blue_Wards_Destroyed'] - df['red_Wards_Destroyed']
df['First_Blood'] = df['blue_First_Blood']
df['Ace_Diff'] = df['blue_Aces'] - df['red_Aces']
df['Assist_Diff'] = df['blue_Assists'] - df['red_Assists']
df['Towers_Destroyed_Diff'] = df['blue_Towers_Destroyed'] - df['red_Towers_Destroyed']
df['Tower_Plates_Diff'] = df['blue_Turret_Plates'] - df['red_Turret_Plates']
df['Inhibitor_Diff'] = df['blue_Inhibitors'] - df['red_Inhibitors']

df['Dmg_Diff'] = df['blue_Total_Dmg'] - df['red_Total_Dmg']
df['Kill_Diff'] = df['blue_Kills'] - df['red_Kills']
df['CS_Diff'] = df['blue_Total_CS'] - df['red_Total_CS']
df['JG_Monster_Diff'] = df['blue_Total_Jungle_Monsters'] - df['red_Total_Jungle_Monsters']
df['Gold_Diff'] = df['blue_Gold_Diff']
df['XP_Diff'] = df['blue_XP_Diff']
df['Dragon_Diff'] = df['blue_Dragons'] - df['red_Dragons']
df['Void_Grub_Diff'] = df['blue_Void_Grubs'] - df['red_Void_Grubs']
df['Herald_Diff'] = df['blue_Heralds'] - df['red_Heralds']

df.drop(columns=['blue_Total_Dmg', 'red_Total_Dmg', 'blue_Kills', 'red_Kills', 'blue_Total_CS', 'red_Total_CS',
                 'blue_Total_Jungle_Monsters', 'red_Total_Jungle_Monsters', 'blue_Gold_Diff', 'blue_XP_Diff',
                 'blue_Dragons', 'red_Dragons','blue_Void_Grubs', 'red_Void_Grubs', 'blue_Heralds', 'red_Heralds',
                 'blue_Wards', 'red_Wards', 'blue_Wards_Destroyed', 'red_Wards_Destroyed', 'blue_First_Blood',
                 'blue_Aces', 'red_Aces', 'blue_Assists', 'red_Assists', 'blue_Towers_Destroyed', 'red_Towers_Destroyed',
                 'blue_Turret_Plates', 'red_Turret_Plates', 'blue_Inhibitors', 'red_Inhibitors'], inplace=True)




Exploratory Data Analysis


Show Code
X = df.drop(columns=['blue_Win'])

# Convert to long format
X_long = X.melt(var_name='Feature', value_name='Value')

g = sns.FacetGrid(
    X_long,
    col="Feature",
    col_wrap=4,
    sharex=False,
    sharey=False,
    height=3.5,
    aspect=1.2
)
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", context="talk")  # bigger fonts

g.map(sns.histplot, "Value", bins=30, color="#4C72B0", edgecolor="black")

g.set_titles("{col_name}")
g.fig.subplots_adjust(top=0.92)
g.fig.suptitle("Distribution of Predictor Variables")

plt.show()









From this plot we can see that Ace_Diff and Inhibitor_Diff very rarely have values other than 0, so we can drop them as they provide little to no value to our training.


Show Code
X = X.drop(columns=['Ace_Diff', 'Inhibitor_Diff'])
df = df.drop(columns=['Ace_Diff', 'Inhibitor_Diff'])

correlation_matrix = df.corr()
target_correlation = correlation_matrix.iloc[1:, 0]
target_correlation = target_correlation.to_frame(name=correlation_matrix.columns[0])
target_correlation = target_correlation.sort_values(by=target_correlation.columns[0], ascending=False)

plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.heatmap(target_correlation, cmap="rocket", annot=True, fmt='.3f')

plt.title("Heatmap of Correlation Between Predictor Variables and 'blue_Win'")
plt.show()









From the heat map we can see the features with the highest correlation with ‘blue_Win’ are Gold_Diff, XP_Diff, Kill_Diff, all with a Pearson correlation coefficient (r) between 0.544 and 0.576, indicating some mild positive correlation.


Show Code
top_features = target_correlation.head(8).index.tolist()
df_top = df[top_features]
top_corr_matrix = df_top.corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.heatmap(top_corr_matrix, cmap="rocket", annot=True, fmt='.2f')

plt.title("Correlation Between Top 8 Features")
plt.show()









Upon checking for multicollinearity we can see that Gold Diff has a very strong correlation with several other variables, we can check the variance inflation factor for our features and review whether to keep “Gold_Diff” (Removing if VIF (Variable Inflation Factor >= 10).


Show Code
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor
import statsmodels.api as sm


X = df.drop(columns=['blue_Win'])

vif_data = pd.DataFrame()
vif_data["feature"] = X.columns
vif_data["VIF"] = [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i) for i in range(X.shape[1])]

print(vif_data.sort_values(by="VIF", ascending=False))


                  feature        VIF
10              Gold_Diff  32.657163
7               Kill_Diff  12.266577
11                XP_Diff   7.473427
4   Towers_Destroyed_Diff   4.455109
3             Assist_Diff   4.187972
8                 CS_Diff   3.274727
5       Tower_Plates_Diff   2.824272
6                Dmg_Diff   2.649275
9         JG_Monster_Diff   1.849424
13         Void_Grub_Diff   1.414245
12            Dragon_Diff   1.255015
2             First_Blood   1.119773
14            Herald_Diff   1.089385
1    Wards_Destroyed_Diff   1.032383
0               Ward_Diff   1.019213


Gold_Diff has a VIF of 32.67 so we’ll remove it for the Logistic Regression dataset and review how its removal affects the VIF of the other features.


Show Code
X = df.drop(columns=['blue_Win', 'Gold_Diff'])
y = df['blue_Win']

vif_data = pd.DataFrame()
vif_data["feature"] = X.columns
vif_data["VIF"] = [variance_inflation_factor(X.values, i) for i in range(X.shape[1])]

print(vif_data.sort_values(by="VIF", ascending=False))


                  feature       VIF
7               Kill_Diff  8.314276
10                XP_Diff  6.747741
3             Assist_Diff  3.421398
4   Towers_Destroyed_Diff  2.817436
6                Dmg_Diff  2.635651
5       Tower_Plates_Diff  2.536428
8                 CS_Diff  2.512260
9         JG_Monster_Diff  1.671427
12         Void_Grub_Diff  1.399061
11            Dragon_Diff  1.248262
2             First_Blood  1.105751
13            Herald_Diff  1.082058
1    Wards_Destroyed_Diff  1.030815
0               Ward_Diff  1.018666


All VIF’s are now below 10 so no further features will be removed. VIF tells how much the variance of a coefficient inflated because it is correlated with other features. A feature with a very large VIF will dominate the model, cause standard errors to blow up, and other coefficients to become unstable, making them flip signs or change drastically, making the model less interpretable. The calculation for VIF is \[VIF_j = 1 / (1-R^2_j)\] Where \[R^2_j\] is from regressing feature j on all other features.


Logistic Regression


Show Code
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1, stratify=y
)

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_scaled = scaler.transform(X_test)

baseline_logistic = LogisticRegression(penalty='l2', max_iter=5000)
baseline_logistic.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)

baseline_pred = baseline_logistic.predict(X_test_scaled)
baseline_prob = baseline_logistic.predict_proba(X_test_scaled)[:, 1]

print("Baseline accuracy:", accuracy_score(y_test, baseline_pred))
print("Baseline ROC AUC:", roc_auc_score(y_test, baseline_prob))


Baseline accuracy: 0.7610969091331629
Baseline ROC AUC: 0.8492581010047242


The baseline Logistic Regression already uses L2 regularization, but the default penalty strength may not be optimal. To check whether stronger or weaker regularization improves the model, we can tune the regularization strength and compare L1, L2, and Elastic Net penalties with 5-fold cross-validation.


Show Code
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline

logistic_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
    ("logistic", LogisticRegression(solver='saga', max_iter=5000))
])

param_grid = [
    {
        "logistic__penalty": ['l1'],
        "logistic__C": [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100]
    },
    {
        "logistic__penalty": ['l2'],
        "logistic__C": [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100]
    },
    {
        "logistic__penalty": ['elasticnet'],
        "logistic__C": [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10],
        "logistic__l1_ratio": [0.2, 0.5, 0.8]
    }
]

grid_search = GridSearchCV(
    logistic_pipeline,
    param_grid=param_grid,
    cv=5,
    scoring='roc_auc',
    n_jobs=1
)

grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("Best parameters:", grid_search.best_params_)
print("Best cross-validated ROC AUC:", grid_search.best_score_)


Best parameters: {'logistic__C': 0.01, 'logistic__l1_ratio': 0.2, 'logistic__penalty': 'elasticnet'}
Best cross-validated ROC AUC: 0.8546027312304298




Show Code
best_logistic = grid_search.best_estimator_
regularized_pred = best_logistic.predict(X_test)
regularized_prob = best_logistic.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

print("Regularized accuracy:", accuracy_score(y_test, regularized_pred))
print("Regularized ROC AUC:", roc_auc_score(y_test, regularized_prob))
print(classification_report(y_test, regularized_pred))


Regularized accuracy: 0.7613293051359517
Regularized ROC AUC: 0.8492243999263761
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

         0.0       0.76      0.77      0.76      2147
         1.0       0.77      0.75      0.76      2156

    accuracy                           0.76      4303
   macro avg       0.76      0.76      0.76      4303
weighted avg       0.76      0.76      0.76      4303



After our grid search with 5-fold cross-validation, we generated a model using Elastic Net with C = 0.01 and l1_ratio = 0.2 suggesting that mild additional regularization may help during cross-validation. However, performance on the held-out test set was nearly identical to the baseline L2 logistic regression with AUC of ~.85 and accuracy of .76 in both cases.

EMR LIS integration diagram


Work in Progress.

title: On-Site Sensor Failure Detection
text:
Inline inspection deformation tool



Problem Introduction
To assess the health and integrity of their pipelines, operators regularly hire inspection companies to run specialized tools known as “pigs” through designated sections of pipe. These tools use various technologies and sensor arrays to detect and measure features in, on, and around the pipe wall.
Occasionally, and perhaps more often than the electrical and mechanical engineers would like to admit, these tools or their sensors fail during an inspection. Field technicians may not know that a failure occurred until the tool is returned and its data is processed. By then, tens or hundreds of miles of inspection data may have been collected and distributed for analysis.
Depending on the number of affected sensors, the type and duration of the failure, and the resulting loss of coverage, the inspection may be partially or entirely invalid. The pipeline may then need to be inspected again, resulting in substantial costs and delays.
In 2024, I was tasked with developing a method to detect these failures on-site, shortly after the tool is removed from the pipeline. This allows technicians to assess whether an inspection is likely to pass validation before leaving the site.


Initial Investigation
To get started, I reached out to several to the leader of field services and the Manager of the Data Analyst teams for a list of runs which were known to contain failed sensors. Once I had a list of ~50 runs, I went through each run to make sure that the failed sensors were marked in a consistent manner. Some were marked from beginning to end, even though it only failed part way through, and there was no difference between a sensor that failed due to electrical noise, and a sensor that flatlined.


Analysis and Solution
Because pipelines vary widely in diameter, coating, and wall thickness, the data had to be normalized if a single model was going to work across inspections. I standardized the signals using median-based scaling and used signal processing to extract features such as signal energy, peak spacing, and related characteristics. Even that was not sufficient, since every run has its own baseline and behavior; what looks like a dead or noisy sensor on one run may be entirely normal on another. To account for this, I added features that compared each sensor’s measurements with the median sensor values at the same location.
The final solution evaluated the pipe in 40-foot segments, generated features for every sensor within each segment, compared those features against the sensor with the median value for each metric, and then used an XGBoost model to predict whether a sensor had failed. To reduce false positives, a section had to be flagged as failed in five consecutive snapshots before it was treated as an actual failure. During training, I held out entire runs for testing, since individual 40-foot sections from the same run were highly correlated.


The Implementation
The implementation also needed to satisfy several technical constraints. It had to run quickly because field technicians and pipeline personnel could not wait hours for results. It also had to operate on the standard Lenovo ThinkPads issued to technicians, without relying on specialized hardware or cloud computing. Finally, each inspection generated billions of sensor readings, while individual tools varied in design and number of sensors. The method therefore needed to process large datasets efficiently while adapting to different tool configurations.
Although I performed the initial analysis and data collection in Python, deploying the solution required porting the pipeline and model to C++ so it could integrate efficiently with our existing infrastructure. Because the model was built with XGBoost, I configured its C++ API within our CMake environment and reimplemented the preprocessing and data collection pipeline in C++. While the model runtime is a function of many variables such as sensor count, sample rate and distance, this change from Python to C++ improved runs that would take over 12 hours to under 10 minutes.

title: Consulting: Automated reporting for Environmental Agency
text:
In March 2026, I was brought in as a consultant for an environmental agency responsible for hazardous-material safety reporting across warehouse facilities. Their team relied on spreadsheet exports that had to be manually reviewed, reorganized, and reformatted into standardized inventory reports, which made the process slow and error-prone.
I built a Python reporting tool that reads the source Excel workbook, extracts the required information from the sheet, groups chemicals by room, and generates a formatted PDF report. The script uses openpyxl to ingest the workbook and reportlab to build the final document, including room-level inventory tables, hazard classifications, quantity summaries by use category, unit handling, pounds conversions, and multi-page pagination logic for longer sections.
Because not every employee is technologically inclined, I wrapped the workflow in a lightweight desktop gui made with Qt6 so staff could select an input workbook through a file explorer and export a finished report without touching a command line.






Figure 1: Example Page

title: EMAT Signal Processing
text:
Work in Progress. Diagrams drawn on Excalidraw as I don’t have access to production data.

Problem Introduction
In inline inspection (ILI), thin cracks and other narrow features can be difficult for magnetic flux leakage (MFL) tools to detect because the magnetic flux may bridge small gaps rather than leak far enough from the pipe wall to produce a measurable signal. Technologies such as electromagnetic acoustic transducers (EMATs) are therefore used to detect and characterize these defects. As the name suggests, these tools transmit a sound wave that travels circumferentially around the wall of the pipe. When these waves reach a crack, the wave is deflected back and can be detected by receivers placed around the tool.
This is the background I was given in early 2026 and asked to create a model to locate where cracks might be. This project was vital as Engineering was developing a larger diameter tool that would contain too many sensors for Analysts to feasibly sort through, even with a Batcave level setup of 12 monitors (we tried!).
The first thing I did was dive into our Confluence pages to read the documentation on the work that had come before me, and reach out to my coworker who had authored the python libraries necessary for analyzing the EMAT run data.






Figure 1: EMAT Tool and Wave Diagram




Transmitter Location: this overly simplified drawing shows the location of the transmitter at 12 o’clock. It is from where the sound wave is generated.
Receiver Location: most receivers are 20 or more degrees offset from the transmitter. The signal data seen in Figure 2 would be from this location.
Direct Transmission (DT): this is the first and typically the largest signal that the receiver will record as the wave travels clockwise around the pipe.
Wrap Around 1 (WA1): this is the the signal recorded as the wave travels counter-clockwise around the pipe. The wave attenuates or becomes weaker as it travels around the pipe, typically it is weaker than DT.
Wrap Around 2 (WA2): As the wave travels clockwise around the pipe, it will eventually return back to the receiver after traveling over 360 degrees.

Any given tool is equipped with 6 or more transmitters, and 1 to 2 receivers per transmitter that record a window of vibrations. As you can see in Figure 1, a transmitter emits a sound wave that will travel both clockwise (yellow) and counter-clockwise (blue) around the pipe.






Figure 2: View of a Single Receiver Signal



Figure 2 shows what the individual sensor data looks like if you plot it over time in R or Python. Using this data from many sensors over a period of time we can reconstruct a visualization of the pipe in 360 degrees.
The sweet spot for detecting echos is between DT and WA1, as by the time the wave reaches WA1, it’s fully circumnavigated the pipe wall and it will begin to attenuate (weaken). Because we are looking for echoes, there’s effectively a blindspot (shown in Figure 3) on the opposite side of the pipe from the receiver and transmitter, where it will have taken too long for the signal to hit a crack and returned a signal.






Figure 3: View of a Single Receiver Over Many Samples



Because we do not know if an echo came from the clockwise or counter-wise direction, we plot a mirror image and super impose them. To ensure background and background noise doesn’t show up as hotspots, I standardized the data and used noise reduction and filtering so that the background would remain as quiet as possible and only real signal would appear as hotspots. to verify a crack was really a crack, and find it’s orientation, I then would take the data from multiple sensors from all angles and overlay and find where the most sensors agreed on a degree position.
As EMAT is a competitive technology in ILI, I will not go into further detail.

title: Jacob Anderson

Some say I’m a Data Scientist, Statistician, Machine Learning Engineer, but I say I model for a living. For the past two years, I’ve worked in the pipeline industry, building applied models that detect cracks and corrosion before they evolve into environmental disasters. In the afternoons I moonlight as course assistant for ASU in Master’s level Biostatistics programming courses (LSC 540, 541).
When I’m not serving corporate overlords, you’ll usually find me playing tabletop games, sports, watching (and quoting) movies, or traveling somewhere new with my wife.

title: A Basic Exploration of Sampling with Mario Party
text:
Introduction and Background
In Mario Party Jamboree, players roll a 10-sided die to determine how far they will travel on their turn. A player can also hold the A button to spin the die faster, also referred to as “charging”. After playing the game a few times I was curious as to how the randomness of the rolls worked decided to explore the question myself. The two questions we are going to explore are:

Are the dice fair (Can they be modeled with a uniform distribution)?

As far as we can tell, the die are fair and a player has an even chance of rolling any number.

Does holding the A button make you roll higher?

Holding the A button does not have a statistically significant effect on roll outcomes.




Show Code
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import chisquare
import numpy as np
data = pd.read_csv("marioparty.csv")

np.random.seed(1)




Sampling
In order to answer these questions, I played a few games of Mario Party with my wife with each of us holding the A buttons, while the NPC’s (Non Player Characters) don’t charge. In total I collected 125 samples, with 59 being charged rolls and 66 uncharged. While there are some effects in the game that control dice outcomes, these have been excluded from the samples.



Charged
Uncharged
Total




59
66
125





Are the Dice Fair?


Show Code
bin_edges = range(1, 12)

plt.hist(data['Number'], bins=bin_edges, color='lightpink', edgecolor='black', align='mid')

plt.title('Histogram of Rolls')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.xlabel('Dice Roll')

bin_centers = [i + 0.5 for i in range(1, 11)]
plt.xticks(bin_centers, labels=range(1, 11))

plt.show()









The rolls have a pretty strong positive-skew with the mode at 1, but outside this peak appears uniform. To analyze, we can perform a Chi-Squared Goodness of Fit Test to see whether or not the observed data fits a uniform distribution.

Chi-Squared Goodness of Fit
\[
H_0: \text {The dice follow a uniform distribution}
\]
\[
H_a: \text {The dice do not follow a uniform distribution}
\]


Show Code
n_faces = 10
observed_freq, bin_edges = np.histogram(data['Number'], bins=n_faces)

total_count = len(data['Number'])
expected_freq = np.full(n_faces, total_count / n_faces)

chi2_stat, p_value = chisquare(f_obs=observed_freq, f_exp=expected_freq)

print(f"Chi-Squared Statistic: {chi2_stat:.2f}")
print(f"P-Value: {p_value:.2f}")


Chi-Squared Statistic: 10.28
P-Value: 0.33


As our P-Value for our Chi-Squared Goodness of Fit test is .33, we fail to reject the null hypothesis as there is insufficient evidence to suggest that the dice do not follow a uniform distribution.



Does Holding the A button make you roll higher?
In order to determine whether or not there is a significant difference between holding the A button and tapping to roll, we’ll analyze our sample set of 66 uncharged rolls and 59 charged rolls (125 total).
\[
H_0: \mu_{chargedDie} = \mu_{unchargedDie}
\]
\[
H_a: \mu_{chargedDie} > \mu_{unchargedDie}
\]


Show Code
charged_rolls = data[data['Charged?'] == 'Yes']['Number']
uncharged_rolls = data[data['Charged?'] == 'No']['Number']

bin_edges = range(1, 12)  # Bins for values 1 through 10
bin_centers = [i + 0.5 for i in range(1, 11)]  # Center of each bin

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 4))

axes[0].hist(charged_rolls, alpha=0.5, bins=bin_edges, color='red', edgecolor='black', label='Charged Rolls', align='mid')
axes[0].set_title(f'Charged Rolls, mean={charged_rolls.mean():.2f}')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Roll Values')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Frequency')
axes[0].set_xticks(bin_centers)
axes[0].set_xticklabels(range(1, 11))  # Label ticks as 1 to 10

axes[1].hist(uncharged_rolls, alpha=0.5, bins=bin_edges, color='green', edgecolor='black', label='Uncharged Rolls', align='mid')
axes[1].set_title(f'Uncharged Rolls, mean={uncharged_rolls.mean():.2f}')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Roll Values')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Frequency')
axes[1].set_xticks(bin_centers)
axes[1].set_xticklabels(range(1, 11))  # Label ticks as 1 to 10

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The distributions appear fairly similar, and charged_rolls has a lower mean (4.97 vs 5.59) which makes me doubt the alternate hypothesis, but I’ll continue with two tests, a Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) test to determine whether the charged_rolls and uncharged_rolls are significantly different and Bootstrap Sampling difference of means between the two distributions and calculating a confidence interval.

Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test
The K-S Test is used here to compare the two distributions and test whether or not they come from the same underlying distribution.


Show Code
from scipy.stats import ks_2samp

ks_statistic, p_value = ks_2samp(charged_rolls, uncharged_rolls)

print(f'K-S Statistic: {ks_statistic:.2f}')
print(f'P-value: {p_value:.2f}')


K-S Statistic: 0.13
P-value: 0.63


With a p-value of 0.63 we fail to reject the null hypothesis as there is insufficient evidence to suggest that the distributions are significantly different.


Bootstrap Sampling
For our Bootstrap Sample we are going to:

Sample both charged_rolls and uncharged_rolls with replacement to create a new sample the same size as the original.
From this we are going to take the mean of charged_rolls and uncharged_rolls
Compute the difference between the mean of the bootstrap samples
Store the difference
Repeat Steps 1-4 n=10,000 times.



Show Code
n = 10000

mean_differences = []

for _ in range(n):
    charged_sample = np.random.choice(charged_rolls, size=len(charged_rolls), replace=True)
    uncharged_sample = np.random.choice(uncharged_rolls, size=len(uncharged_rolls), replace=True)
    
    charged_mean = np.mean(charged_sample)
    uncharged_mean = np.mean(uncharged_sample)
    
    mean_differences.append(charged_mean - uncharged_mean)

mean_differences = np.array(mean_differences)

lower_bound = np.percentile(mean_differences, 2.5)
upper_bound = np.percentile(mean_differences, 97.5)

plt.hist(mean_differences, bins=30, alpha=0.7, color='skyblue', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Bootstrap Distribution of Difference in Means (Charged - Uncharged)')
plt.xlabel('Difference in Means')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')

plt.axvline(x=lower_bound, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'2.5% CI: {lower_bound:.2f}')
plt.axvline(x=upper_bound, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f'97.5% CI: {upper_bound:.2f}')

plt.legend()
plt.show()









As the 95% confidence interval of our bootstrapped difference in means contains 0, we reject the null hypothesis as there is insufficient evidence to suggest that charged dice produce higher rolls than uncharged dice.



Conclusion and Limitations
All three (Chi-Squared GOF, Kolmogorov-Smirnov, and Bootstrap DOF) tests performed resulted in an insignificant p-value, meaning that we did not have sufficient evidence to suggest that the the dice aren’t fair or able to be modeled accurately by a uniform distribution, and that holding the A button to charge the dice does not give any advantage.
To more accurately test this in the future, I could collect more samples, as well as samples from other human players as all charged samples came from humans and all uncharged samples came from NPC’s (Non-Player Characters). (This was done because we didn’t have extra controllers, and the NPC’s don’t charge dice).

title: Settlers of Catan Pip Calculator
text:
I wanted a lightweight way to sketch a Settlers of Catan board and immediately see which settlement spots are strongest by raw pip count. This first draft runs entirely in the browser with Shinylive, so there is no server behind it.
You can edit the 19 hexes, paste a JSON board state, and compare every vertex based on the adjacent number tokens.
Open the dashboard in a new tab
